paper.title_en: Cosmic ray experiment and simulation of Ce3+–doped high gadolinium-based fluoro–oxide glass scintillators
paper.abstract_en: Ce3+–doped gadolinium-based fluoro–oxide (GFO) glass was developed for prospective high-energy physics (HEP) experiment applications. The minimum ionizing particle (MIP) response and timing characteristics of GFO glasses coupled with a silicon photomultiplier (SiPM, 3*3 mm2) were investigated using a cosmic-ray (muon) coincidence setup. The MIP response of a 1 cm-thick glass is 142.1 ± 1.7 p.e., satisfying the criteria for hadron calorimeter applications. As the thickness increases, the MIP responses decrease to 112.7 ± 0.4 (2 cm-thick) and 76.8 ± 1.4 p.e./cm (4 cm-thick). Additionally, Cosmic-ray-induced scintillation shows a dominant slow decay component of ∼500 ns which increases with glass thickness. In this work, appropriately increasing the trigger area remains an effective method to improve the trigger efficiency. According to Monte Carlo simulation, the MIP responses are close to the experimental values, maintaining errors below 6%. The simulated MIP response reaches a maximum value of 361.7 ± 2.7 p.e. for a 5 cm thickness, owing to the short attenuation length of GFO glass which leads to significant photon absorption. Therefore, enhancing its light attenuation length to reduce light loss during internal transmission is currently a critical objective.
